introduction: Time and Work problems measure how quickly people, machines, or pipes complete a fixed job. The core idea is work rate: if A finishes alone in 10 days, A completes 1/10 of the job per day. Combined rates add when workers cooperate, and efficiency ratios compare how fast different agents work on the same task.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Treat the whole job as 1 unit. If A completes work in a days, A's one-day work = 1/a. If B takes b days, B's one-day work = 1/b. Working together, combined one-day work = 1/a + 1/b, so together they finish in ab/(a+b) days.

If A is twice as efficient as B, A takes half the time B takes for the same work. Efficiency is inversely proportional to time when work is constant.

Man-days (or man-hours): Work ∝ Men × Days × Hours. More workers or more days mean more total work capacity.

Pipes and cisterns mirror time and work: an inlet filling in x hours fills 1/x of the tank per hour; an outlet emptying in y hours removes 1/y per hour. Net rate = sum of inlet rates minus outlet rates.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Project staffing, factory production planning, scheduling maintenance crews, and water-tank filling with multiple pipes use these models.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Adding times instead of adding rates (e.g., 6 and 12 days ≠ 18 days together).
2) Forgetting to subtract an outlet pipe's rate.
3) Ignoring that efficiency doubles means time halves.
4) Not converting hours to days consistently in man-hour problems.
5) Assuming (a+b) days when two people take a and b days individually.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Together time = ab/(a+b) for two workers with individual times a and b.
• Work done = Rate × Time; Rate = Work / Time.
• If A:B efficiency = m:n, then A:B time = n:m for the same work.
• Remaining work after t days = 1 − (rate × t).
• Alternate-day work: sum work done on odd and even days separately.

formulas.title: One-day work
formulas.content: If A finishes in a days, one-day work of A = 1/a

formulas.title: Combined two workers
formulas.content: Together time = ab / (a + b); Combined one-day rate = 1/a + 1/b

formulas.title: Work relation
formulas.content: Work = Rate × Time; Rate = Work / Time

formulas.title: Efficiency and time
formulas.content: For same work, Efficiency × Time = constant; if efficiency ratio is m:n, time ratio is n:m

formulas.title: Man-days
formulas.content: M1 × D1 × H1 / W1 = M2 × D2 × H2 / W2 (same work proportion)

formulas.title: Pipes net rate
formulas.content: Net filling rate = Σ(inlet rates) − Σ(outlet rates); time to fill = 1 / net rate

shortcutTips.title: Product over sum
shortcutTips.tip: Two workers with times a and b alone finish together in ab/(a+b) days.

shortcutTips.title: LCM method
shortcutTips.tip: Assign total work as LCM of individual times, then find per-day work units.

shortcutTips.title: Efficiency flip
shortcutTips.tip: Double efficiency halves the required time for the same job.

shortcutTips.title: Outlet subtract
shortcutTips.tip: An emptying pipe contributes a negative rate—subtract it from inflow.

shortcutTips.title: Remaining work
shortcutTips.tip: After t days together, leftover = 1 − t × (combined daily rate).

shortcutTips.title: Three workers
shortcutTips.tip: Add all individual daily rates: 1/a + 1/b + 1/c gives combined daily work.

solvedExamples.problem: A can finish a work in 12 days and B in 18 days. In how many days do they finish together?
solvedExamples.solution: They finish together in 7.2 days (36/5 days).

solvedExamples.problem: A is twice as good a worker as B. If B alone takes 24 days, how long does A take alone?
A pipe fills a tank in 6 hours and another empties it in 8 hours. With both open, how long to fill the empty tank?
If 12 men can complete a job in 15 days, how many men are needed to finish it in 10 days?
